title: Near-Earth asteroids discovered over time
subtitle: Cumulative number of discovered asteroids with an orbit that brings them into proximity with Earth. This is broken down by size of the asteroid.
caption:
Near-Earth asteroids discovered over time

Cumulative number of discovered asteroids with an orbit that brings them into proximity with Earth. This is broken down by size of the asteroid.

The stacked area chart tracks the cumulative count of Near-Earth asteroids discovered from 1990 through 2025, categorized by physical dimensions. The vertical axis measures the total number of objects, ranging from zero to 40,000, while the horizontal axis marks time in five-year increments. The data defines Near-Earth asteroids as those with a closest approach to the Sun of less than 1.3 astronomical units, where one unit equals the distance from Earth to the Sun. Three distinct size classes form the layers of the visualization: the bottom black layer represents asteroids larger than 1 kilometer; the middle purple layer covers those between 140 meters and 1 kilometer; and the top orange layer comprises objects smaller than 140 meters. The total count remains near zero in 1990 and rises slowly until roughly 2005, after which the curve steepens significantly. By 2025, the cumulative total reaches the 40,000 mark. The largest category by volume is the group smaller than 140 meters, which expands rapidly after 2010 to constitute the majority of the total height. The middle category, between 140 meters and 1 kilometer, shows steady growth, reaching a cumulative count of approximately 11,000 to 12,000 by the end of the period. The smallest group in terms of count, those larger than 1 kilometer, forms a thin, flat band at the base, indicating that nearly all objects in this size class were already identified by 2010 with minimal additions thereafter. The visual pattern shows an accelerating rate of discovery for smaller objects coinciding with a plateau in the detection of the largest asteroids.
